title: Insights
text:
Holiday-heavy months are intentionally modeled to show higher sales volume, creating a realistic opportunity for inventory and campaign planning.



Discount bands can increase quantity sold, but profit impact should be monitored at the category and product level.



High-frequency customers create a concentrated share of revenue, making retention and loyalty segmentation valuable.

title: Insights
text:
Holiday-heavy months are intentionally modeled to show higher sales volume, creating a realistic opportunity for inventory and campaign planning.



Discount bands can increase quantity sold, but profit impact should be monitored at the category and product level.



High-frequency customers create a concentrated share of revenue, making retention and loyalty segmentation valuable.

title: Insights
text:
Business Recommendations

Prioritize inventory planning for high-revenue categories before holiday demand spikes.


Use RFM segmentation to separate champions, loyal customers, at-risk customers, and new or promising customers.


Launch win-back campaigns for high-value customers with long purchase gaps.


Compare regional category winners to localize merchandising and promotional strategy.


Monitor discount bands by both revenue and profit so promotions increase volume without eroding margin.

title: Insights
text:
Resume Bullets

Analyzed 50,000+ retail transactions by developing 20+ SQL queries using joins, CTEs, window functions, and aggregations to uncover customer behavior, revenue trends, and business insights.
Automated a PostgreSQL analytics pipeline with Python, pandas, SQLAlchemy, and Faker to generate synthetic retail data, create normalized tables, load CSV files, and export reusable query results.
Built an interactive Streamlit business intelligence dashboard and Quarto portfolio case study with KPI cards, Plotly visualizations, filters, business recommendations, and recruiter-ready documentation.

title: Insights
text:
About This Project
This project was designed to mirror the type of work expected from a data analyst or data science intern: translating ambiguous business questions into structured data, SQL analysis, reproducible automation, dashboarding, and executive communication.
The case study is intentionally end-to-end. It demonstrates data generation, schema design, SQL analytics, Python automation, dashboard UX, and presentation quality in one cohesive portfolio project.

title: Customer Spending Analytics Dashboard
text:
Portfolio Case Study



A professional retail analytics project analyzing 50,000 synthetic transactions to uncover revenue trends, customer behavior, product performance, store performance, and business opportunities.

View Dashboard View GitHub Repository Read the SQL Analysis

title: Customer Spending Analytics Dashboard
text:
Project Overview
This project simulates a real business analytics workflow for a retail company. It starts with synthetic operational data, loads the data into a normalized PostgreSQL database, answers stakeholder questions with SQL, exports reusable analysis outputs with Python, and presents the final insights in an interactive Streamlit dashboard.
The portfolio deliverable includes both a working analytics application and this Quarto case study, so recruiters and interviewers can quickly understand the business problem, technical approach, and decision-making value.

title: Customer Spending Analytics Dashboard
text:
Business Problem
Retail leaders need to know where growth is coming from, which customers are worth retaining, how product categories perform, and whether discounts are improving revenue without damaging profitability. The goal of this analysis is to convert transaction-level data into recommendations for merchandising, retention, regional strategy, and dashboard-driven decision-making.

title: Customer Spending Analytics Dashboard
text:
What Makes This Portfolio-Ready


Business Framing
The project is organized around realistic stakeholder questions: revenue trends, churn risk, category performance, regional performance, discount impact, and customer value.


Technical Depth
The SQL analysis demonstrates joins, CTEs, window functions, RFM segmentation, cohort retention, moving averages, rankings, and aggregation logic.


Executive Presentation
The dashboard and Quarto website turn raw analysis into a recruiter-friendly case study with KPIs, visuals, recommendations, and resume bullets.

title: Dashboard
text:
The Streamlit dashboard is designed as an executive-facing business intelligence tool. It includes KPI cards, filters, charts, customer tables, product analysis, store rankings, and business recommendations.

Customer Analytics
Top customers, RFM segment distribution, and churn-risk customers.


Product Analytics
Top products, revenue by category, and profit margin by category.


Store Analytics
Revenue by region and store performance rankings.


Recommendations
Filtered, business-facing actions based on the current dashboard view.

title: Dashboard
text:
UX Design Notes
The dashboard is organized for a business user who needs quick answers: high-level KPIs first, then trend context, then customer, product, and store drilldowns. Filters are kept in the sidebar so the main page remains focused on analysis.

title: Analysis
text:
The dataset is generated with Python and Faker to resemble realistic retail behavior. It intentionally includes repeat customers, weighted best-selling products, holiday seasonality, and discount-driven quantity effects.



customers

{.metric} Demographics, signup date, location, and loyalty tier.





products

{.metric} Category, subcategory, price, and cost.





stores

{.metric} Store metadata and sales region.





transactions

{.metric} Purchase date, quantity, discount, payment method, revenue, and profit.

title: Analysis
text:
The dataset is generated with Python and Faker to resemble realistic retail behavior. It intentionally includes repeat customers, weighted best-selling products, holiday seasonality, and discount-driven quantity effects.



customers

{.metric} Demographics, signup date, location, and loyalty tier.





products

{.metric} Category, subcategory, price, and cost.





stores

{.metric} Store metadata and sales region.





transactions

{.metric} Purchase date, quantity, discount, payment method, revenue, and profit.

title: Analysis
text:
Database Schema
The PostgreSQL schema uses normalized tables with primary keys, foreign keys, NOT NULL constraints, check constraints, and indexes on transaction date plus customer, product, and store IDs.





erDiagram
    customers ||--o{ transactions : makes
    products ||--o{ transactions : includes
    stores ||--o{ transactions : records

    customers {
        int customer_id PK
        varchar first_name
        varchar last_name
        varchar email
        varchar gender
        int age
        varchar city
        char state
        date signup_date
        varchar loyalty_tier
    }

    products {
        int product_id PK
        varchar product_name
        varchar category
        varchar subcategory
        numeric unit_price
        numeric cost
    }

    stores {
        int store_id PK
        varchar store_name
        varchar city
        char state
        varchar region
    }

    transactions {
        int transaction_id PK
        int customer_id FK
        int product_id FK
        int store_id FK
        date transaction_date
        int quantity
        numeric unit_price
        numeric discount_percent
        varchar payment_method
        numeric revenue
        numeric profit
    }

title: Analysis
text:
SQL Analysis
The SQL file answers 26 business questions. The queries are written for PostgreSQL and demonstrate a range of techniques expected in analytics roles.


Revenue Analytics
Monthly revenue, profit, average order value, month-over-month growth, running revenue, and 3-month moving averages.


Customer Analytics
Repeat customer rate, customer lifetime value, RFM segmentation, churn-risk detection, cohort retention, and declining spend flags.


Product and Store Analytics
Category revenue, highest-margin products, product rankings, regional performance, and store rankings within each region.

title: Analysis
text:
SQL Techniques Demonstrated

INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN
Aggregations with GROUP BY and HAVING
Single and multiple CTEs
Window functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, SUM OVER, and moving averages
CASE statements for segmentation and business logic
Date functions for monthly, seasonal, weekday/weekend, and cohort analysis
Customer segmentation with RFM scoring

title: Analysis
text:
Example Business Questions

Which customers generate the highest lifetime revenue?
Which product categories are most profitable?
Which customers are at risk of churn?
Which stores lead revenue within each region?
How do discounts affect order volume, revenue, and profit?
Are revenue trends improving month over month?
